name: Rounding and Estimation — About How Many?
whatIsIt: Rounding replaces a number with a nearby FRIENDLY number that is easier to think with: 47 becomes 50, 82 becomes 80. Estimation uses these friendly numbers to get a quick 'about how many' answer. It is not cheating — it is smart thinking that checks exact answers!
whyNeeded: You rarely need exact numbers instantly. Will ₹100 be enough for a ₹47 toy and a ₹38 book? Round: 50 + 40 = 90 — yes! Estimation also GUARDS your exact answers: if you compute 47 + 25 = 612, the estimate (about 75) shouts that something went wrong.
realLifeUses: Shopping: quickly checking if the money in your pocket is enough
Time: 'about 30 minutes to school' instead of 27 minutes
Crowds: 'about 500 people at the match' — nobody counts each head!
Checking homework: an estimate catches wild wrong answers instantly

vocabulary.meaning: Replace a number with the nearest friendly ten or hundred.
A quick 'about' answer using rounded numbers.
The friendly ten a number is closest to: 47 → 50, 42 → 40.
Exactly in the middle (like 45)? Round UP.

story.title: Milestones on the Village Road
story.text: Walking the dusty village road, Ravi passes a milestone marked 47. 'About how far — nearer 40 or 50?' asks Thatha. On the number line in his mind, Ravi checks: '47 is only 3 steps from 50, but 7 steps from 40. So it rounds to 50 — the nearer ten.' 'Rounding gives a quick, close-enough answer,' nods Thatha.
story.answerInStory: 50 — on the number line, 47 is only 3 steps from 50 but 7 steps from 40.

visual.caption: Think of 40 to 50 as a number line. 47 stands here — closer to the 50 end. So 47 rounds to 50.

teachingGallery.title: Round to the nearest ten — which ten is closer?
teachingGallery.note: 5 or more rounds UP; less than 5 rounds DOWN.

examples.caption: 47 is past halfway (7) → nearest ten is 50.
42 is before halfway (2) → nearest ten is 40.
45 sits on the halfway mark → round UP to 50.

standardMethod.summary: Look at the ones digit: 5 or more rounds UP, 4 or less rounds DOWN.
steps: Find the two friendly tens around your number: 47 sits between 40 and 50.
7 is 5-or-more → round UP to 50. (If it were 4-or-less, round DOWN.)
Exactly 5 in the ones (like 45)? The halfway rule says round UP: 45 → 50.

alternateMethods.name: Number-line method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE why a number rounds where it does.
steps: Draw the two friendly tens as the ends of a line.
Whichever end is closer — that's the round!
alternateMethods.example: 82 between 80 and 90: only 2 steps from 80 → rounds to 80.

alternateMethods.name: Estimation guard (checking answers)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: After ANY calculation, to catch wild mistakes.
steps: Round each number in the problem.
Compare: your exact answer should be CLOSE to the estimate.
alternateMethods.example: 47 + 38: estimate 50 + 40 = 90. Exact 85 ✓ close. An answer like 815 ✗ fails the guard!

workedExamples.problem: Anu has ₹100. A toy costs ₹47 and a book ₹38. WITHOUT exact adding, is her money enough?
steps: Round both prices UP to be safe: 47 → 50, 38 → 40.
Even rounding UP, the total is under ₹100 — so YES, enough, with change!
workedExamples.answer: Yes — about ₹90, which is less than ₹100.

trickPractice.intro: To round, peek at just ONE digit — the one to the right — then up or down.

commonMistakes.mistake: Rounding 47 down to 40 because '4 is the first digit'.
commonMistakes.fix: The ONES digit decides, not the tens! Look at the 7: it is 5-or-more, so 47 climbs UP to 50. Check on a number line — 47 is clearly closer to 50.

commonMistakes.mistake: Rounding 45 down to 40 — 'it's in the middle, so either way'.
commonMistakes.fix: Halfway numbers follow ONE agreed rule so everyone gets the same answer: exactly 5 in the ones rounds UP. 45 → 50, always.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing the estimate as the final exact answer (47 + 38 = 90).
commonMistakes.fix: An estimate is a helper, not the answer! Say it with 'about': 'about 90'. The exact answer (85) still needs real adding — the estimate just checks it.

hintLadder: 62 sits between which two friendly tens?
Look at the ones digit: 2. Is it 5-or-more, or 4-or-less?

medium.q: Round 45 to the nearest ten. (Careful — the halfway house!)
hintLadder: Exactly 5 in the ones → UP, always.

medium.q: Estimate first: about how much is 47 + 38? (Give the estimate, not the exact sum.)
medium.explain: About 90 (the exact answer 85 lives close by — that's the point!).

challenge.q: Ravi computed 52 + 46 = 998. Captain Count caught it in two seconds WITHOUT adding. How?

options.label: He estimated: about 50 + 50 = 100, so 998 is impossible
He is just very fast at exact adding

challenge.answer: He estimated: about 50 + 50 = 100, so 998 is impossible
hintLadder: The Captain never added exactly — he used friendly numbers.
Round 52 and 46 and add in your head.
About 100. Can the true answer be near 998?

challenge.q: A school trip needs 'about 30' seats per bus. 27 children from Class A and 31 from Class B are going. ESTIMATE the total children (nearest ten each, then add).
hintLadder: Round 27 to the nearest ten: ones digit 7 → ?

questions.q: Which digit decides whether a number rounds up or down to the nearest ten?
hintLadder: Which digit tells you how far past the friendly ten you are?
In 47, which digit made it round UP?

questions.q: Quick guard check: estimate 58 + 33 using friendly tens.

teachBackPrompt: You are the new About-Bridge keeper! Explain to someone at home: how do you round 47 and 82 to the nearest ten, what happens with 45, and show how estimation caught Ravi's silly 998.

revisionCard.summary: Rounding: ones digit 5-or-more → UP, 4-or-less → DOWN, exactly halfway → UP (45→50). Estimation = friendly-number quick answer, said with 'about'. Use it as your answer's bodyguard: 47+38 ≈ 90, so 85 ✓ and 815 ✗.

gameMission.title: The About-Bridge Crossings
gameMission.brief: Travellers keep arriving with exact numbers! Round each one to open the bridge, and use estimates to catch the impostors carrying impossible answers.

realLifeProject: Estimation shopping game: before a parent totals the grocery bill, round each item's price and predict the total. How close was your 'about' answer to the real bill? Whoever gets within ₹20 wins!
